video_title: Scaling Laws: Lawyering on the Frontier with Janel Thamkul
gist: Janel Thamkul, who built Anthropic's Frontier Legal Team after leading legal for Google Research, argues copyright law is the wrong tool for AI's real problems (economic displacement and incentives to keep creating), and that the underserved frontier issues are job-displacement safety nets, AI's energy/environmental supply chain, and AI's impact on cognitive development. She frames frontier AI lawyering as a translation layer between researchers and regulators that must push legal boundaries without 'yolo' ignoring precedent.

themes.theme: Copyright vs. AI: the real fight is elsewhere

insights.claim: Copyright law is the wrong legal instrument for the AI-and-creators dispute; the real underlying issue is economic displacement, which is broader than copyright and will hit lawyers, financial professionals, and many industries, not just creatives.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Signals a frontier-lab legal view that copyright litigation against AI labs is misframed and that the durable policy battle will shift to economy-wide displacement remedies; watch for labs steering settlements/policy away from copyright precedent toward broader compensation schemes.
insights.quote: I think economic displacement is an issue that's broader than copyright. It's broader than creatives.

insights.claim: Copyright-based remuneration claims are not aligned with how content is actually used to train a model, making copyright a poorly-fitted 'hammer' for one niche of a much larger problem.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: A former Anthropic frontier lawyer arguing training use is mechanistically distinct from copying supports labs' fair-use posture; relevant to litigation risk pricing for AI labs and to rights-holders' leverage.
insights.quote: I don't think it is aligned with how content is actually used in training a model.

insights.claim: The second, distinct policy problem is preserving incentives for humans to keep creating; the concern (echoing Judge Chhabria in the Meta case) is not that Taylor Swift is harmed but that future Taylor Swifts won't write songs if AI dilutes the market.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Separating 'displacement compensation' from 'creation incentives' reframes where regulation and lab policy will land; investors in generative-media and licensing platforms should watch for incentive/subsidy mechanisms rather than pure copyright enforcement.
insights.quote: this is going to impact all the future Taylor Swifts and make future Taylor Swifts not write songs um because AI will you know dilute the market

insights.claim: Stock-photography-style commercial creators face genuine economic displacement from generative AI, whereas creators who don't depend on art for livelihood are largely insulated because their value is in the process, not the output.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Identifies commoditized commercial content (stock imagery) as the most exposed creative segment to AI substitution; a sector-level short/avoid signal versus process-driven or brand-driven creative work.
insights.quote: someone who is like making a living off of generating stock photography is in a very different position

insights.claim: Google Books is the seminal copyright precedent for AI training, and outcomes are playing out as anticipated even before ChatGPT.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Reinforces that AI-training fair-use defenses lean on Google Books; a key anchor for assessing litigation exposure of any model developer using copyrighted corpora.
insights.quote: in AI for copyright in particular Google books is you know seinal precedent that you have to know and we're seeing it play out uh in ways that we anticipated

themes.theme: Underpriced frontier risks: jobs, energy, cognition

insights.claim: Society is behind on building social safety-net systems for AI-driven job displacement, and the standard 'upskilling/re-education' playbook is hollow because it doesn't define what value the displaced worker actually gains.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: A frontier-lab insider flagging upskilling as inadequate suggests labs/policymakers will eventually back direct transfer or redistribution mechanisms (consistent with lab proposals on wealth distribution); watch policy risk for sectors automating headcount.
insights.quote: Oh, upskilling, uh, you know, re, you know, re-education so that people understand how to use AI. To what end

insights.claim: AI's compute buildout depends on supply-chain and energy inputs with significant community and ecosystem impacts, and the US is behind on clean energy and on the environmental rules needed to balance AI energy demand against climate and local impacts.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Energy and permitting are framed as a binding constraint and a coming regulatory front for AI scaling; relevant to power, clean-energy, and data-center infrastructure exposure and to siting/regulatory risk.
insights.quote: I am a little worried that we are behind on clean energy, uh, and having the right environmental rules and regulations

insights.claim: A distinct and underdiscussed risk is AI's impact on cognitive development for middle- and high-schoolers who are still learning how to think critically, as opposed to its effect on already-skilled adults.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Foreshadows a regulatory/reputational front around AI use by minors in education; relevant to edtech product design and to policy risk for consumer AI aimed at students.
insights.quote: I'm worried about the use of AI for middle schoolers or high schoolers, people who are in pro like in places where they're trying to learn how to do cognitive thinking

insights.claim: AI sentiment outside the West Coast tech bubble is largely fearful and negative, driven by job-displacement anxiety amplified by big-tech layoffs of tens of thousands, whether or not those layoffs are actually due to AI automation.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Negative public sentiment is a tailwind for restrictive AI regulation and a political-risk factor for labs; useful as a leading indicator for policy backlash regardless of layoff causation.
insights.quote: there's a lot of fear around AI, a lot of negative sentiment, particularly around job displacement

themes.theme: Frontier AI legal function as competitive infrastructure

insights.claim: Anthropic was about 100 people when Thamkul joined in 2023, and she built out its entire legal function (commercial, product counsel, litigation and regulatory, plus interim employment counsel and patent prosecution) before specializing the team on a Frontier AI Council.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Concrete marker of Anthropic's 2023 scale and how recently its legal/governance infrastructure was assembled; useful context for assessing the maturity of the lab's regulatory and IP defenses.
insights.quote: came to Anthropic and left Google in 2023 came to Anthropic company was about a hundred people at the time

insights.claim: Effective frontier AI lawyering requires knowing precedent well enough to analogize but refusing to be wedded to it: push the boundaries of risk deliberately rather than 'yolo' ignoring constraints, and steer rather than block development.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Describes the operating philosophy of a frontier lab's legal team as risk-steering rather than risk-avoidance, which predicts labs will keep testing legal gray zones; relevant to how aggressively labs will litigate and ship.
insights.quote: You have to be willing to push the boundaries but you can't just yolo push the boundaries.

insights.claim: The core value of a non-technical frontier lawyer is acting as a bidirectional translation layer between highly technical researchers and non-technical policymakers/regulators; over-indexing on technical depth erodes that translation ability.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: A reusable hiring/operating model for AI-governance roles that values translation over STEM credentials; informs how labs and firms staff the regulator-facing function that will shape AI rules.
insights.quote: your role as a lawyer, as a non-technical person, is to be that translation layer between a highly technical group and non-technical people like policy makers

insights.claim: Anthropic has formally worked on novel legal questions including AI model personhood/legal personhood for technology, model welfare/sentience, and AI's impact on rule of law and concentration of power.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: Confirms a leading lab is allocating legal resources to far-future governance questions; an early signal of where novel AI legal frameworks and lab policy proposals (personhood, model welfare) may originate.
insights.quote: at anthropic I've had the privilege to think about uh model personhood, legal personhood for technology

insights.claim: Anthropic and OpenAI have both recently put forward bold policy proposals including new schemes of wealth distribution and mechanisms for managing the economic transition.
insights.speaker: Kevin (host)
insights.so_what: Frontier labs themselves are floating redistribution mechanisms, signaling they expect significant labor disruption and are pre-positioning on the policy response; watch for these proposals to shape regulation affecting their own business models.

themes.theme: Practitioner playbook: low-ego curiosity as an edge

insights.claim: In a field where AI development changes daily/weekly/monthly, senior lawyers' reluctance to admit knowledge gaps becomes a liability; institutionalizing a 'hall pass' for new hires to re-ask how training is done keeps the team current.
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: A management insight for fast-moving technical domains: seniority that discourages questions degrades accuracy; relevant to how legal/compliance teams keep pace with model development.
insights.quote: the last time we were talking about how we do this training I thought it was this but I think it's evolved

insights.claim: Equating the quality of creative or knowledge work with the quantity of time invested is becoming anachronistic; conceptual value and the elegance of the idea matter more than 'sweat of the brow.'
insights.speaker: Janel Thamkul
insights.so_what: A mental model for valuing AI-augmented output by idea quality rather than labor hours; relevant to how billing, pricing, and productivity get reframed across cognitive-work industries.
